text:
In 1972, Erich Clar introduced his aromatic π-sextet rule, providing a qualitative yet powerful framework for understanding the aromatic character of benzenoid systems. Over four decades later, this deceptively simple model remains a vital source of inspiration and a cornerstone for researchers in the field. Its utility has been extensively validated through both experimental observations—such as X-ray crystallography, NMR spectroscopy, and advanced microscopy (STM/AFM)—and rigorous theoretical computations. 

In this review, we examine the enduring success of Clar’s rule by highlighting its application across diverse chemical environments. We specifically focus on its ability to predict the local aromaticity and reactivity of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), and we discuss two significant recent applications: the analysis of resonance-assisted hydrogen bonding and the justification of the emerging biradical character in higher acenes. Despite some inherent limitations, Clar’s rule continues to be an essential tool for navigating the complexities of sp2-bonded carbon materials.
